planting.note: Root hardwood cuttings in late winter or plant container-grown plants in moist, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun to part shade; American black currant is one of the most shade-tolerant fruiting shrubs available and will produce fruit even in substantial shade — a valuable quality for woodland edible gardens. It requires moist soil conditions and tolerates wet soils better than most fruiting shrubs.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun to full shade; American black currant is a native fruiting shrub found in moist woodlands and streambanks from Nova Scotia to Virginia and west to the Rockies, producing clusters of small, round, black-purple currants in July–August that are excellent for jams, jellies, juice, and wine. The fruit flavor is similar to European black currant but milder, without the strong 'cassis' musk of Ribes nigrum, making it more palatable to those who find European black currant too strong. Most importantly, it is one of very few fruiting shrubs that produces in deep shade, making it irreplaceable for woodland edible gardens where blueberries and most other fruit shrubs fail. The fragrant, small, white to yellowish tubular flowers in spring are attractive to hummingbirds and native bees. The foliage is attractively lobed and resinous-scented when brushed. American black currant is alternate host for white pine blister rust and should not be planted within 900 feet of white pines in susceptible regions — check with local extension before planting.

planting.note: Root softwood cuttings in early summer or plant container-grown plants in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part shade; climbing hydrangea is very slow to establish — it may appear not to grow for the first 2–3 years while developing its root system. After establishment it grows steadily and becomes long-lived and self-sustaining. It climbs by aerial rootlets and requires no additional support on rough surfaces like brick, stone, or wood.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full shade to part shade; climbing hydrangea is among the most valuable vines for shaded positions — one of the few vigorous, ornamental climbers that succeeds and blooms in substantial shade. It clings to walls, tree trunks, and structures by tiny adhesive aerial rootlets without damaging sound mortar and masonry. In June and July, established plants produce large, flat-topped flower heads (lace-caps) up to 10 inches across — creamy white with ornamental sterile flowers surrounding a center of tiny, fertile flowers — in breathtaking abundance that is one of the showiest June spectacles possible on a shaded wall. The bark on mature stems exfoliates in thin, cinnamon-red sheets, creating beautiful winter interest. The broadly oval, glossy leaves turn bright yellow in fall. Native to Japan and Korea, it is slow to begin growing after planting (famously described as 'the first year it sleeps, the second year it creeps, the third year it leaps') but is exceptionally long-lived and essentially maintenance-free once established on a suitable structure.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in moist, well-drained, adaptable soil in full sun to part shade; American bittersweet requires both male and female plants for fruit production — plant at least one male per 3–5 females, or select self-fertile cultivars. Do NOT plant Oriental bittersweet (Celastrus orbiculatus), which is invasive — verify you have the native species from a reputable native plant nursery.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, adaptable soil in full sun to part shade; American bittersweet is a native deciduous twining vine prized for its spectacular fall and winter fruit display — clusters of small, round capsules that split open in autumn to reveal brilliant orange-red arils (fleshy seed coverings), creating a vivid orange-and-red effect that persists on the vine through winter and is widely used in dried arrangements and holiday decoration. The small, greenish-yellow flowers in early summer are inconspicuous but attract small native bees. The fruit is a critical winter food source for many songbirds including bluebirds, robins, cedar waxwings, and mockingbirds, which eat the arils while dispersing the seeds. American bittersweet is increasingly rare in wild populations because Oriental bittersweet (C. orbiculatus), an invasive Asian relative, has displaced it across much of the East — purchase only from reputable native plant nurseries and verify the species. Female plants require a nearby male for fruit set. An outstanding native vine for trellises, fences, and naturalistic settings where its wildlife value can be fully expressed.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown plants in moist, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; Goldflame honeysuckle prefers cool roots and appreciates a mulch layer. Unlike Japanese honeysuckle (L. japonica), it does not spread aggressively and is well-behaved in the garden — twining habit requires a trellis or support.

directions: Train on a trellis, arbor, or fence in moist, well-drained, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; Goldflame honeysuckle is a hybrid ornamental vine producing exceptionally showy flowers from late spring through summer — the tubular flowers are an intense coral-red to orange-red on the outside with a creamy-yellow interior, presenting a striking bicolor combination as the buds open and the flowers age. The fragrance is moderate to strong, sweet and honeysuckle-like. Unlike the invasive Japanese honeysuckle (L. japonica), Goldflame is a controlled, twining vine that blooms repeatedly through the season and attracts hummingbirds and butterflies throughout its long bloom period. The foliage is glaucous blue-green, semi-evergreen in mild climates, and attractive in its own right. Particularly valuable for hummingbird gardens as it provides a succession of nectar-rich flowers through most of the warm season. Well-behaved and garden-appropriate for zones 4–9, it thrives even in heat if given consistent moisture and partial shade protection in the afternoon in the hottest climates.

planting.note: Root semi-hardwood cuttings in summer or plant container-grown plants in almost any soil in full sun to full shade; Boston ivy climbs by adhesive disc-tipped tendrils that adhere to virtually any rough surface without additional support. It will cover walls rapidly once established and is difficult to remove from masonry — site carefully. The cultivar 'Veitchii' has smaller, more finely textured leaves that turn brilliant crimson.

directions: Plant in almost any soil in full sun to full shade; Boston ivy is the quintessential self-clinging wall vine of ivy-covered colleges and historic buildings, native to China and Japan and widely planted worldwide for its ability to cover brick, stone, and concrete walls with a seamless carpet of foliage. The simple, three-lobed leaves (variable in shape) are overlapping and smooth-textured throughout summer, then transform in autumn to some of the most spectacular fall colors of any vine — brilliant crimson, scarlet, and burgundy, rivaling the finest maples in intensity. Unlike English ivy, Boston ivy is deciduous — letting winter light through the wall and providing summer cooling by shading the masonry while becoming bare in winter. It climbs by tiny, disc-tipped adhesive tendrils that grip masonry firmly without growing into mortar. The small blue-black berries in fall are an important food source for birds. Unlike its cousin Virginia creeper (P. quinquefolia), Boston ivy's three-lobed leaves have a slightly smoother texture and the vine tends to stay flatter against a wall.
